questions.question: Which areas do you want to discuss for Parallel Execution Engine?

options.label: Worker pool design
options.description: How workers are created, assigned phases, and recycled. One ClaudeService per phase or reusable pool? (DependencyScheduler.getReady() feeds this — already built)

options.label: CLI flag semantics
options.description: How --parallel, --concurrency N, and --parallel 2,3,5 interact. Default concurrency cap? (Existing CLI has --phases flag for range selection)

options.label: Git worktree lifecycle
options.description: Branch naming, worktree location, merge-back strategy, cleanup timing. (No existing worktree code — built from scratch)

options.label: Sequential compatibility
options.description: How parallel mode coexists with sequential. Shared orchestrator code or separate paths? (Current sequential loop at orchestrator line 375)

answers.Which areas do you want to discuss for P: Worker pool design, CLI flag semantics, Sequential compatibility, Git worktree lifecycle

data.message: [Answer] Which areas do you want to discuss for Parallel Execution Engine? -> Worker pool design, CLI flag semantics, Sequential compatibility, Git worktree lifecycle
All four areas selected. Let's dive in.

questions.question: How should workers be assigned to phases?

options.label: One worker per phase (Recommended)
options.description: Spawn a new ClaudeService for each ready phase, up to concurrency limit. Simple, no reuse complexity. Worker dies when phase completes.

options.label: Reusable pool
options.description: Pre-create N ClaudeService instances that pick up phases as they finish. More complex but potentially faster startup for later phases.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the approach based on ClaudeService startup cost and implementation complexity.

data.message: [Answer] How should workers be assigned to phases? -> One worker per phase (Recommended)

questions.question: When the concurrency limit is reached and a phase finishes, how should the next phase be assigned?

options.label: Immediate dispatch (Recommended)
options.description: As soon as a worker finishes and scheduler.markComplete() returns newly eligible phases, dispatch the next one immediately.

options.label: Batch dispatch
options.description: Wait until all currently running phases finish, then dispatch the next batch of ready phases together.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what produces better throughput and simpler code.

data.message: [Answer] When the concurrency limit is reached and a phase finishes, how should the ne... -> Immediate dispatch (Recommended)

questions.question: Where should per-worker metadata (worker ID, worktree path, PID, phase number) be tracked?

options.label: In-memory only (Recommended)
options.description: WorkerPool tracks workers in memory. If autopilot crashes, resume logic re-detects state from disk (worktrees, phase completion markers).

options.label: Persisted to state.json
options.description: Write worker metadata to state.json alongside phase state. Enables precise resume after crash but adds write complexity.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on crash recovery needs and state complexity.

data.message: [Answer] Where should per-worker metadata (worker ID, worktree path, PID, phase number... -> In-memory only (Recommended)

questions.question: How should questions from parallel phases be routed to the dashboard?

options.label: Phase-tagged queue (Recommended)
options.description: All questions go to a shared queue but tagged with phase number. Dashboard shows phase context. One answer file, orchestrator routes answers.

options.label: Per-worker question files
options.description: Each worker has its own questions-phase-{N}.json file. Dashboard polls all files. More isolated but more file I/O.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on existing IPC patterns and dashboard complexity.

data.message: [Answer] How should questions from parallel phases be routed to the dashboard? -> Phase-tagged queue (Recommended)

questions.question: How should --parallel work when no phase list is given?

options.label: All eligible phases (Recommended)
options.description: --parallel with no arguments runs all phases from the roadmap, using the DependencyScheduler to determine which can run concurrently.

options.label: Require explicit phases
options.description: --parallel always requires a phase list (e.g., --parallel 2,3,5). Running --parallel alone is an error.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the most intuitive CLI behavior.

data.message: [Answer] How should --parallel work when no phase list is given? -> All eligible phases (Recommended)

questions.question: What should the default concurrency cap be?

options.label: 3 workers (Recommended)
options.description: Default --concurrency 3. Each ClaudeService uses ~200-500MB. Keeps memory reasonable on most machines.

options.label: 2 workers
options.description: Conservative default. Safer for lower-spec machines but slower throughput.

options.label: Auto-detect
options.description: Base on available CPU cores or memory. More adaptive but harder to predict behavior.

data.message: [Answer] What should the default concurrency cap be? -> 3 workers (Recommended)

questions.question: How should --parallel interact with the existing --phases flag?

options.label: Combine them (Recommended)
options.description: --phases 2-5 --parallel runs phases 2-5 in parallel (respecting dependencies). --phases alone stays sequential. --parallel alone runs all.

options.label: --parallel takes a list directly
options.description: --parallel 2,3,5 selects AND enables parallel. --phases is ignored when --parallel has arguments.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the most ergonomic combination.

data.message: [Answer] How should --parallel interact with the existing --phases flag? -> Combine them (Recommended)

questions.question: Should --parallel be a persistent config option (config.json) or CLI-only?

options.label: CLI flag only (Recommended)
options.description: Parallel mode is opt-in per run. User explicitly chooses each time. Simpler, no surprise behavior.

options.label: Also in config.json
options.description: Can set parallel: true in config so --resume also runs parallel. More convenient for repeated use.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on resume semantics and user safety.

data.message: [Answer] Should --parallel be a persistent config option (config.json) or CLI-only? -> CLI flag only (Recommended)

questions.question: How should the orchestrator handle the parallel vs sequential code paths?

options.label: Unified loop (Recommended)
options.description: One orchestrator loop uses the scheduler always. Sequential mode = concurrency 1 (one phase at a time). Parallel mode = concurrency N. Same code path, different limit.

options.label: Separate paths
options.description: Keep the existing sequential for-loop unchanged. Add a parallel execution path that only activates with --parallel. More code but zero risk to existing behavior.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on code complexity and regression risk.

data.message: [Answer] How should the orchestrator handle the parallel vs sequential code paths? -> Unified loop (Recommended)

questions.question: In sequential mode (no --parallel), should phases still use git worktrees?

options.label: No worktrees in sequential (Recommended)
options.description: Sequential mode runs phases directly in the main repo, exactly as today. Worktrees are a parallel-only concept. Zero behavior change for existing users.

options.label: Always use worktrees
options.description: Even sequential phases run in worktrees for consistency. Adds overhead but simplifies the merge path (always the same).

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on backward compatibility requirements.

data.message: [Answer] In sequential mode (no --parallel), should phases still use git worktrees? -> No worktrees in sequential (Recommended)

questions.question: How should --resume work when the previous run was parallel?

options.label: Resume as sequential (Recommended)
options.description: --resume without --parallel resumes remaining phases sequentially. User must explicitly add --parallel to resume in parallel mode.

options.label: Remember mode from state
options.description: State records whether the run was parallel. --resume automatically uses the same mode. Could surprise the user.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on safety and predictability.

data.message: [Answer] How should --resume work when the previous run was parallel? -> Resume as sequential (Recommended)

questions.question: Should the unified loop use the DependencyScheduler even for the sequential --phases range case?

options.label: Always use scheduler (Recommended)
options.description: Even --phases 2-5 without --parallel feeds through the scheduler (concurrency=1). Consistent code path, respects dependency ordering.

options.label: Scheduler only for parallel
options.description: Sequential uses simple numeric ordering. Scheduler only activates with --parallel. Less risk but two code paths.

questions.question: Where should worktrees be created on disk?

options.label: Adjacent to repo (Recommended)
options.description: Create worktrees at ../{repo}-worktrees/phase-{N}/ next to the main repo. Avoids nested .git issues and keeps repo clean.

options.label: Inside .planning/
options.description: Create at .planning/worktrees/phase-{N}/. Contained within project but adds to .planning/ bloat.

options.label: System temp directory
options.description: Use OS temp dir (e.g., /tmp/gsd-worktree-{hash}-{N}/). Clean separation but harder to find for debugging.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on git worktree best practices and cleanup ergonomics.

data.message: [Answer] Where should worktrees be created on disk? -> Adjacent to repo (Recommended)

questions.question: What branch naming convention for worktree branches?

options.label: gsd/phase-{N} (Recommended)
options.description: e.g., gsd/phase-2, gsd/phase-3. Clear namespace, easy to identify GSD branches. Branched from current HEAD.

options.label: gsd/autopilot/{timestamp}/phase-{N}
options.description: Includes run timestamp to avoid conflicts across runs. More verbose but guarantees uniqueness.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks a clean convention that avoids conflicts.

data.message: [Answer] What branch naming convention for worktree branches? -> gsd/phase-{N} (Recommended)

questions.question: When should worktree changes be merged back to the main branch?

options.label: Immediately on phase complete (Recommended)
options.description: As soon as a phase finishes successfully, merge its worktree branch back to main. Keeps main up-to-date, surfaces conflicts early.

options.label: All at end
options.description: Wait until all parallel phases complete, then merge all worktree branches in dependency order. Defers conflicts but risks big merge at the end.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on conflict resolution timing and Phase 3 requirements.

data.message: [Answer] When should worktree changes be merged back to the main branch? -> Immediately on phase complete (Recommended)

questions.question: What should happen if a merge fails (conflict) in Phase 2? (Phase 3 adds auto-resolution)

options.label: Fail the phase, preserve worktree (Recommended)
options.description: Mark the phase as failed, keep the worktree for manual resolution. Log the conflict. Phase 3 will add auto-resolution later.

options.label: Abort entire run
options.description: Treat merge conflict as fatal — stop all workers. Conservative but safe.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what gives the best Phase 3 upgrade path.

data.message: [Answer] What should happen if a merge fails (conflict) in Phase 2? (Phase 3 adds auto... -> Fail the phase, preserve worktree (Recommended)

questions.question: Which areas do you want to discuss for Failure Handling and Git Conflict Resolution?

options.label: Fail-fast vs continue semantics
options.description: How --continue blocks dependents, what state tracking is needed, how failures are reported to the user. (Currently: only fail-fast via requestShutdown() — no --continue support yet)

options.label: Graceful shutdown behavior
options.description: How SIGINT/SIGTERM cleans up all workers and worktrees. (ShutdownManager exists but doesn't call WorkerPool.abortAll() — only aborts the orchestrator's own ClaudeService)

options.label: Merge conflict auto-resolution
options.description: What conflicts can be auto-resolved, what strategy to use, how to generate resolution reports. (mergeWorktree() currently just returns false on conflict — no resolution attempted)

options.label: Failed worktree preservation
options.description: What to preserve for debugging, how to communicate failure context, cleanup of preserved worktrees on next run. (Worktrees already preserved on merge failure — needs user-facing affordance)

questions.question: Which areas do you want to discuss for Failure Handling and Git Conflict Resolution?

options.label: Merge conflict auto-resolution
options.description: How aggressive should auto-resolution be? Git strategies (ours/theirs/union) vs AI-assisted resolution via Claude. Which conflicts auto-resolve vs flag for manual intervention? (mergeWorktree() currently just returns false on any conflict)

options.label: --continue mode semantics
options.description: When a phase fails with --continue, how are dependent phases handled? What about partial failures (phase succeeded but merge failed)? How is the user notified of blocked vs skipped phases?

options.label: Graceful shutdown behavior
options.description: ShutdownManager exists but doesn't clean up worktrees or wait for in-flight merges. How aggressive should SIGINT cleanup be? Kill immediately vs let current step finish? (FAIL-03, FAIL-04)

options.label: Resolution report format
options.description: What should merge conflict resolution reports contain and where are they stored? How are prior reports fed as context for future merges in the same run? (GIT-04, GIT-05)

questions.question: Which areas do you want to discuss for Failure Handling and Git Conflict Resolution?

options.label: Failure modes
options.description: Fail-fast vs --continue behavior. How should dependent phases react when a dependency fails? What info does the user see on failure? (ShutdownManager and requestShutdown() already exist — this extends them)

options.label: Graceful shutdown
options.description: SIGINT/SIGTERM cleanup behavior. Should failed worktrees be preserved? How are child ClaudeService processes terminated? (ShutdownManager has LIFO handlers — this wires WorkerPool into them)

options.label: Merge conflict resolution
options.description: Auto-resolution strategy for worktree merges. What conflicts can be auto-resolved vs need manual intervention? How is the resolution report structured? (mergeWorktree() currently just returns false on conflict)

options.label: Resolution context chaining
options.description: How do resolution reports from earlier merges inform later conflict resolution in the same run? What gets captured and passed forward? (GIT-05 requirement)

answers.Which areas do you want to discuss for F: Failure modes, Graceful shutdown, Resolution context chaining, Merge conflict resolution

data.message: [Answer] Which areas do you want to discuss for Failure Handling and Git Conflict Reso... -> Failure modes, Graceful shutdown, Resolution context chaining, Merge conflict resolution
All four areas selected. Let's dive in.

questions.question: When a phase fails in --continue mode, how should dependent phases be handled?

options.label: Skip dependents (Recommended)
options.description: Dependent phases are automatically skipped with a clear message. Independent phases keep running. Simplest and safest — no risk of running a phase on incomplete foundations.

options.label: Queue dependents
options.description: Dependent phases stay queued (not skipped). After the run, user sees which phases couldn't start and why. Allows re-running with --resume after fixing the failed phase.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the approach during implementation based on what fits the scheduler best.

data.message: [Answer] When a phase fails in --continue mode, how should dependent phases be handled? -> Skip dependents (Recommended)

questions.question: What should the failure output look like when a phase fails?

options.label: Summary table (Recommended)
options.description: A table at run end showing all phases with status (passed/failed/skipped/running). Failed phases show error reason. Similar to test runner output — scannable at a glance.

options.label: Inline log only
options.description: Error logged immediately when failure occurs. No summary at end. User reads the log to piece together what happened.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks output format based on what existing logging patterns support.

data.message: [Answer] What should the failure output look like when a phase fails? -> Summary table (Recommended)

questions.question: In fail-fast mode (default), should currently-running workers finish their current step before stopping, or abort immediately?

options.label: Abort immediately (Recommended)
options.description: Call abortAll() on WorkerPool to kill in-progress ClaudeService processes. Fastest shutdown. Worktrees preserved for debugging.

options.label: Finish current step
options.description: Let running workers complete their current lifecycle step (e.g., finish 'plan' if mid-plan). More graceful but slower — a stuck step could block shutdown.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what ClaudeService.abortCurrent() supports cleanly.

data.message: [Answer] In fail-fast mode (default), should currently-running workers finish their cu... -> Abort immediately (Recommended)

questions.question: Should the --continue flag also be configurable via config.json, or CLI-only like --parallel?

options.label: CLI-only (Recommended)
options.description: Like --parallel, user explicitly opts in each run. Fail-fast is always the safe default. Consistent with Phase 2 pattern.

options.label: Configurable
options.description: Allow workflow.continue_on_failure in config.json. Users who always want continue behavior don't have to pass the flag every time.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on consistency with existing flag patterns.

data.message: [Answer] Should the --continue flag also be configurable via config.json, or CLI-only ... -> CLI-only (Recommended)

data.message: [Answer] More questions about failure modes, or move to next? -> Next area

questions.question: On SIGINT/SIGTERM, should all worker worktrees be cleaned up, or preserved for inspection?

options.label: Clean up successful, preserve failed (Recommended)
options.description: Workers that completed successfully get their worktrees cleaned. Failed or in-progress worktrees are preserved so the user can inspect or resume. Matches FAIL-04 requirement.

options.label: Preserve all
options.description: Keep all worktrees on shutdown regardless of status. User manually cleans up. Safest but leaves clutter.

options.label: Clean up all
options.description: Remove all worktrees on shutdown. Clean state but loses debugging context for failed phases.

data.message: [Answer] On SIGINT/SIGTERM, should all worker worktrees be cleaned up, or preserved fo... -> Clean up successful, preserve failed (Recommended)

questions.question: How should child ClaudeService processes be terminated during shutdown?

options.label: SIGTERM then force kill (Recommended)
options.description: Send SIGTERM to each child process, wait a short timeout (e.g., 5s), then SIGKILL any survivors. Gives Claude Code a chance to clean up gracefully.

options.label: Immediate SIGKILL
options.description: Kill all child processes immediately. Fastest but no cleanup opportunity for Claude Code processes.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what Node.js child_process supports cross-platform (especially Windows).

data.message: [Answer] How should child ClaudeService processes be terminated during shutdown? -> SIGTERM then force kill (Recommended)

questions.question: Should a double Ctrl+C force immediate exit (skip cleanup), or always run the full shutdown sequence?

options.label: Double = force exit (Recommended)
options.description: First Ctrl+C starts graceful shutdown. Second Ctrl+C within a few seconds forces immediate process.exit(1) with no cleanup. Familiar pattern from many CLI tools.

options.label: Always graceful
options.description: Every Ctrl+C runs the full shutdown sequence. If stuck, user must kill -9 from another terminal.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what ShutdownManager already supports.

data.message: [Answer] Should a double Ctrl+C force immediate exit (skip cleanup), or always run the... -> Double = force exit (Recommended)

questions.question: After graceful shutdown completes, should the process exit with code 0 or non-zero?

options.label: Non-zero (1) (Recommended)
options.description: Exit 1 when shutdown was triggered by failure or signal. Makes it CI-friendly — pipelines detect the interrupted run.

options.label: Context-dependent
options.description: Exit 0 if all completed phases succeeded (some just didn't run). Exit 1 only if a phase actually failed. More nuanced but harder to reason about.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks exit code semantics based on CI best practices.

data.message: [Answer] After graceful shutdown completes, should the process exit with code 0 or non... -> Non-zero (1) (Recommended)

options.label: More questions

data.message: [Answer] More questions about graceful shutdown, or move to next? -> Next area

questions.question: What types of conflicts should be auto-resolved vs flagged for manual intervention?

options.label: Disjoint files only (Recommended)
options.description: Auto-resolve when phases touched completely different files (git can merge cleanly with retry). If same file has conflicting changes, mark phase as failed and preserve worktree. Conservative and predictable.

options.label: Disjoint + non-overlapping hunks
options.description: Also auto-resolve when phases edited different parts of the same file (non-overlapping hunks). More aggressive — handles most real cases but risk of semantic conflicts.

options.label: Full auto with AI assist
options.description: Use Claude to analyze and resolve even overlapping conflicts. Most powerful but expensive (extra API calls) and harder to trust for correctness.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the resolution strategy based on what git merge supports natively.

data.message: [Answer] What types of conflicts should be auto-resolved vs flagged for manual interve... -> You decide

questions.question: When a merge conflict is resolved (auto or manual), what should the resolution report contain?

options.label: Structured file (Recommended)
options.description: A JSON or markdown file per merge with: conflicting files, resolution strategy used, before/after snippets, and which phase's changes won. Machine-readable for context chaining (GIT-05).

options.label: Log entries only
options.description: Conflict details logged to the event stream. No separate file. Simpler but harder to use as context for future merges.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks report format based on what's most useful for downstream consumption.

data.message: [Answer] When a merge conflict is resolved (auto or manual), what should the resolutio... -> Structured file (Recommended)

questions.question: Where should merge resolution reports be stored?

options.label: Phase directory (Recommended)
options.description: Store at .planning/phases/XX-name/merge-report.md alongside other phase artifacts. Each phase owns its merge outcome. Easy to find and review.

options.label: Central merges directory
options.description: Store all reports in .planning/merges/phase-{N}-report.md. All merge history in one place. Better for cross-phase comparison.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks location based on how it integrates with existing .planning/ structure.

data.message: [Answer] Where should merge resolution reports be stored? -> Phase directory (Recommended)

questions.question: If a merge fails and can't be auto-resolved, should the run continue (in --continue mode) with remaining phases, or stop merges entirely?

options.label: Continue, skip merge-blocked (Recommended)
options.description: The failed-merge phase is marked failed. Other completed phases still attempt their merges (serialized). Maximizes progress — one bad merge doesn't block unrelated phase merges.

options.label: Stop all merges
options.description: Once any merge fails, no further merges are attempted. All remaining worktrees preserved. Safest but may leave good work unmerged.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on git safety considerations.

data.message: [Answer] If a merge fails and can't be auto-resolved, should the run continue (in --co... -> Continue, skip merge-blocked (Recommended)

questions.question: More questions about merge conflict resolution, or move to next?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move on to Resolution Context Chaining

options.label: More questions
options.description: Dig deeper into merge resolution behavior

data.message: [Answer] More questions about merge conflict resolution, or move to next? -> Next area

questions.question: How should resolution reports from earlier merges be made available to later merge attempts in the same run?

options.label: In-memory accumulator (Recommended)
options.description: The orchestrator maintains a list of resolution reports in memory during the run. When a new merge is attempted, prior reports are passed as context. No disk I/O overhead. Reports are also written to disk for post-run review.

options.label: Read from disk each time
options.description: Each merge reads all existing report files from .planning/phases/*/merge-report.md before attempting resolution. Simpler (no in-memory state) but slower with filesystem overhead.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the context passing mechanism based on what's simplest.

data.message: [Answer] How should resolution reports from earlier merges be made available to later ... -> In-memory accumulator (Recommended)

questions.question: Should resolution context from a previous run (on disk) be loaded when starting a new run with --resume?

options.label: Yes, load prior reports (Recommended)
options.description: On --resume, read existing merge-report.md files from disk and seed the in-memory accumulator. Gives continuity across interrupted runs.

options.label: No, fresh each run
options.description: Each run starts with empty context. Reports from previous runs exist on disk for human review but aren't fed back in. Simpler and avoids stale context.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on how --resume works with the rest of the system.

data.message: [Answer] Should resolution context from a previous run (on disk) be loaded when starti... -> Yes, load prior reports (Recommended)

questions.question: What information from a resolution report is most useful as context for resolving future conflicts?

options.label: Files + strategy used (Recommended)
options.description: Which files conflicted, what resolution strategy was applied (e.g., 'took phase 3 changes for src/worker/'), and the outcome. Enough to pattern-match without being overwhelming.

options.label: Full diffs
options.description: Include complete before/after diffs of resolved conflicts. Maximum context but could be very large and noisy for later merges.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the level of detail based on what's practical for context window constraints.

data.message: [Answer] What information from a resolution report is most useful as context for resol... -> Files + strategy used (Recommended)

questions.question: Should the resolution context be surfaced to the user in the summary table at run end?

options.label: Yes, brief merge column (Recommended)
options.description: Summary table includes a 'Merge' column showing: clean/resolved/conflict for each phase. Users who want details check the report file.

options.label: No, reports only
options.description: Summary table shows pass/fail status. Merge details only in the report files. Keeps the table clean.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the level of merge visibility in the summary.

data.message: [Answer] Should the resolution context be surfaced to the user in the summary table at... -> Yes, brief merge column (Recommended)

questions.question: Which areas do you want to discuss for Dashboard and Event Stream Integration?

options.label: Event stream consolidation
options.description: How per-worker event files merge into a single SSE stream. EventTailer currently tails one file — needs to handle N worker files. (EVNT-03)

options.label: Per-phase status display

answers.Which areas do you want to discuss for D: Event stream consolidation, Per-phase status display, Live progress visualization, Question routing by phase

data.message: [Answer] Which areas do you want to discuss for Dashboard and Event Stream Integration? -> Event stream consolidation, Per-phase status display, Live progress visualization, Question routing by phase
All four areas selected. Let's dive into each one.

questions.question: How should the EventTailer discover worker event files?

options.label: Directory polling (Recommended)
options.description: EventTailer scans the log/ directory for events-phase-*.ndjson files on each tick. Auto-discovers new workers as they start. No orchestrator coordination needed.

options.label: Orchestrator registration
options.description: Orchestrator explicitly tells the EventTailer which files to tail via an API call when a worker starts/stops. More precise but requires coupling.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the best approach based on the existing architecture.

data.message: [Answer] How should the EventTailer discover worker event files? -> Directory polling (Recommended)

questions.question: Should the consolidated stream preserve the original per-worker event files, or merge them into a single events.ndjson?

options.label: Read-only fan-in (Recommended)
options.description: EventTailer reads all per-worker files and fans them into a single SSE stream. Worker files remain untouched. Simple, no write contention.

options.label: Merge into single file
options.description: A consolidator process merges per-worker files into one events.ndjson. Single file for tailing but adds write coordination.
